faq.q: Why do the glaciers matter for California's water?
faq.a: About half of California's fresh water comes from the Sierra Nevada, much of it from snowmelt and glacier melt. Glaciers act as high-elevation reservoirs, storing winter snow as ice and releasing it gradually through summer melt to sustain streams like the Tenaya, Merced, and Tuolumne. As glaciers shrink, July and August streamflow will gradually decline and cold-water aquatic ecosystems will be stressed.

title: Why giant sequoias thrive where other trees burn
dek: The same fires that kill every other tree in the Sierra are the ones the giant sequoia depends on. Two-foot bark, embedded tannins, and a seedling ecology that fails without burning.
seoDek: The fires that kill every other Sierra tree are the ones giant sequoias depend on. Two-foot bark, embedded tannins, seedlings that need burning.
placeholder: The Wawona Tunnel Tree, Mariposa Grove (fell 1969)

faq.q: Why do giant sequoias need fire?
faq.a: Fire clears competing vegetation and exposes the bare mineral soil that tiny sequoia seeds need to establish, and it opens the canopy to give seedlings light. In a fire-suppressed forest, shade-tolerant species like white fir colonize the understory, the canopy closes, and sequoia seedlings are starved of light. A sequoia grove without fire is a grove in slow decline.

faq.q: How do giant sequoias survive fires that kill other trees?
faq.a: Mature sequoia bark can be two feet thick, loose and fibrous so it barely conducts heat, and rich in tannin that resists flame. When fire passes, the outer bark chars and that charring insulates the living wood beneath. Thinner-barked trees like white fir and sugar pine can be killed by ten or twenty minutes of sustained ground-fire heat, while the sequoia survives with only a scar.

faq.q: How often did fire burn in the Mariposa Grove?
faq.a: A 1929 fire-scar survey documented in Nature Notes counted at least two major burns per century over the past two thousand years. Trees that germinated in the 1600s grew in a forest that burned roughly every ten to thirty years; a single two-thousand-year-old sequoia may carry eight or ten distinct fire scars.

faq.q: Why did Yosemite change its fire policy?
faq.a: For about 150 years the park tried to prevent all fire, which let fuel accumulate and shade-tolerant species crowd the understory, leaving groves unable to regenerate and primed for hotter, more destructive fires. Land managers now treat fire as a necessity and run carefully timed, low-intensity restoration burns in groves like Mariposa, Tuolumne, and Merced to mimic the natural fire regime.

title: Hetch Hetchy: the Yosemite Valley you didn't know you skipped
dek: Same elevation. Same length. Same kind of granite. Carved by the same kind of glacier as the famous one, and still mostly empty of visitors. Why almost no one goes, and why you should.
seoDek: Same elevation, same length, same granite as Yosemite Valley. Carved by the same glaciers and still mostly empty. Why no one goes, and why you should.
placeholder: Wapama Falls in spring snowmelt

faq.q: How do I get to Hetch Hetchy in Yosemite?
faq.a: Take Highway 120 west to Evergreen Road (about 1 mile past the Big Oak Flat entrance station), then Hetch Hetchy Road 16 miles to O'Shaughnessy Dam. The road has a gate with set hours, so check nps.gov/yose before driving out. The round trip from Yosemite Valley is about 3-4 hours including driving.

faq.q: Can you hike to Wapama Falls at Hetch Hetchy?
faq.a: Yes. The Wapama Falls trail is 4.8 miles round trip from the dam, gaining about 500 feet. In peak snowmelt (April-May) the crossing at the falls can be knee-deep and is sometimes closed. In summer it's a dry footbridge crossing. The falls are dramatically higher in May than in August.

faq.q: Is Hetch Hetchy crowded?
faq.a: Far less than Yosemite Valley. On a busy weekend, Hetch Hetchy sees a fraction of Valley traffic. The gated road hours limit early-morning surge further. If the Valley is slammed, Hetch Hetchy offers a wild Yosemite experience with essentially no wait.

faq.q: Why was Hetch Hetchy Valley flooded?
faq.a: In 1913 Congress passed the Raker Act authorizing O'Shaughnessy Dam, despite John Muir's national campaign to protect the valley. San Francisco needed a reliable water supply, and the valley's remote location inside federal land made it the proposed reservoir site. Muir called it a desecration equal to flooding the Sistine Chapel.

faq.q: Can Hetch Hetchy be restored?
faq.a: Engineering studies show the valley could be drained and would recover ecologically within decades. The political challenge is substantial: San Francisco still draws water and hydroelectric power from the reservoir. Replacement infrastructure would take years and billions of dollars. Restoration legislation is periodically introduced in Sacramento.

title: Yosemite stargazing: where to look up, and when
dek: On a moonless August night at Olmsted Point, the Milky Way doesn't look like a thin band. It casts shadows. Where to go, when to go, and how to see the sky the way our ancestors did.
seoDek: On a moonless August night at Olmsted Point, the Milky Way casts shadows. Where to go, when to go, and how to see Yosemite the way the ancients did.
placeholder: Milky Way over Sentinel Dome, July

faq.q: Where is the best stargazing in Yosemite?
faq.a: Olmsted Point and Glacier Point are the most accessible high-altitude sites, both above 7,000 feet, away from Valley light, with wide sky exposure. Tuolumne Meadows has longer horizon lines. Sentinel Dome is excellent for Milky Way photography. The Valley floor has noticeably more light pollution than any of these.

faq.q: When is the best time to stargaze in Yosemite?
faq.a: New moon weekends from late July through September. The Milky Way core is highest in August, which is also the driest, warmest month for high-country nights. Use a moon phase calendar and timeanddate.com moonrise tables to find nights when the moon sets before midnight.

faq.q: Is there a Yosemite Star Party?
faq.a: Yes. The Yosemite Conservancy organizes annual stargazing programs at Glacier Point, typically in late summer, with telescopes and astronomy interpreters. Tickets are limited and sell out early, so check yosemite.org for dates and registration.

faq.q: Can you see the Milky Way from Yosemite Valley?
faq.a: On a moonless night, yes, but the Valley's lodging and village lighting reduce contrast. The high-country sites, Olmsted Point, Glacier Point, and Sentinel Dome, offer dramatically better views with comparable drive time from most gateway towns.

faq.q: What should I bring for stargazing in Yosemite?
faq.a: Warm layers (high-country nights drop to 40F even in August), a red-light flashlight to preserve night vision, a blanket or camp chair for lying back, and binoculars, which reveal far more than the naked eye without telescope setup. A free app like Stellarium helps identify constellations and planets.

title: Yosemite for non-hikers: the park you can experience without a trail
dek: Yosemite is built for non-hikers more thoroughly than almost any park in the country. A complete visit is possible without ever putting on hiking boots. Here's how to plan one.
seoDek: Yosemite is built for non-hikers more thoroughly than almost any national park. A complete visit without ever putting on hiking boots. Plan one.
placeholder: Tunnel View from the overlook parking

faq.q: Is Yosemite worth visiting if you don't hike?
faq.a: Absolutely. The most dramatic viewpoints, Tunnel View, El Capitan Meadow, Valley View, Glacier Point, and Olmsted Point, are all drive-up or involve short paved walks under half a mile. The Valley tram tour, Mariposa Grove tram, and in-park shuttle require no hiking.

faq.q: What can you see in Yosemite without hiking?
faq.a: Tunnel View (drive-up), El Capitan and Yosemite Falls from roadside meadows, Bridalveil Fall (0.5-mile paved path), Lower Yosemite Fall loop (1-mile paved loop), Valley View (pull-out), Glacier Point overlook (drive-up), Olmsted Point (Tioga Road pull-out), and Mariposa Grove (tram tour available).

faq.q: Are there guided tours in Yosemite for non-hikers?
faq.a: Yes. The Yosemite Valley Floor Tour is a 2-hour open-air tram covering the main Valley viewpoints. Glacier Point bus tours run daily in summer. The Mariposa Grove tram offers a narrated circuit through the sequoia grove. All are operated by the park concessionaire, bookable at yosemite.com.

faq.q: Is Glacier Point accessible without hiking?
faq.a: Yes. Glacier Point is a drive-up viewpoint with a paved overlook and restrooms. No hiking is required to reach the main viewpoint. In summer, a bus tour from the Valley also serves it for those who do not want to drive the narrow 16-mile road.

faq.q: Can you visit Yosemite by bus or train?
faq.a: Yes. Amtrak's San Joaquin line runs to Merced, where YARTS buses make the 2-hour trip to Yosemite Valley. YARTS runs year-round on the Highway 140/Merced route. In summer, YARTS also connects Lee Vining and Mammoth Lakes to the Valley via Tioga Road.

title: How to pack your car for a Yosemite trip
dek: Nobody writes about packing the car. But the car is the base camp for most Yosemite trips, and what's in it decides whether a flat tire is an inconvenience or a crisis.
seoDek: Nobody writes about packing the car. But the car is the base camp for most Yosemite trips, and what's in it decides crisis from inconvenience.

faq.q: What should I pack in my car for a Yosemite trip?
faq.a: A well-packed cooler, a rigid water jug of four to seven gallons, layers (fleece, rain jacket, warm hat) for every person, a paper park map and offline maps, and a roadside kit with a portable jump starter, spare tire, and flashlight. Binoculars, a windshield sunshade, and camp chairs earn their space too.

faq.q: Do I need to store food in my car in Yosemite because of bears?
faq.a: Yes. Any food or scented item, including sealed coolers, granola bars, gum, sunscreen, and chapstick, must be stored properly. In campgrounds it goes in the bear box. At trailheads keep it in the trunk, out of sight, with windows closed. Bears recognize coolers through windows and peel doors open.

faq.q: How long will a cooler stay cold in Yosemite?
faq.a: Three to four days if it stays shut and in the shade in the trunk. A cooler that is opened every thirty minutes and sits in the back seat in July lasts about eight hours. Pack in layers and freeze water bottles to use as ice blocks and cold drinking water.

faq.q: Is there cell service in Yosemite for navigation?
faq.a: Not reliably outside the Valley. Carry the free paper park map and download offline maps over wifi before you enter. There is no gas between Lee Vining and Crane Flat, about 60 miles, so fill up before entering from any direction.

faq.q: What roadside gear should I bring to Yosemite?
faq.a: A portable jump starter (dead batteries are common at elevation), a spare tire with the tools and the know-how to change it, a basic tool kit with duct tape and zip ties, and a real flashlight that works when your phone is dead. Tows can be hours away.

title: Yosemite gateway towns compared: which one to base in
dek: Pick the wrong gateway town and you'll burn hours of every day on the road. Pick the right one and the rest of the trip gets easier. A side-by-side from someone who's stayed in all five.
seoDek: Pick the wrong Yosemite gateway and you'll burn hours on the road. A side-by-side of Mariposa, Oakhurst, Groveland, El Portal, Lee Vining.

faq.q: Which Yosemite gateway town is closest to the Valley?
faq.a: El Portal, about 14 miles and 25-30 minutes from Yosemite Valley via Highway 140. Mariposa is next at 45 miles and 45-60 minutes. Lee Vining is closest to Tuolumne Meadows (about 30 minutes) but 90+ minutes from the Valley.

faq.q: Which Yosemite gateway town is best for first-time visitors?
faq.a: Mariposa, for most first-timers. It pairs a real town (groceries, dinner, lodging at several price points) with the reliable, year-round Highway 140 approach. El Portal is closer if you can get a room, but Mariposa is the better all-round balance of cost and convenience.

faq.q: Which Yosemite gateway town is cheapest?
faq.a: Groveland and Mariposa typically have the widest price range and the most budget options. El Portal lodging is priced like in-park lodging because of the location. Oakhurst has predictable chain prices. Lee Vining has limited inventory and is priced accordingly.

faq.q: Which Yosemite gateway town is best for families?
faq.a: Mariposa, by a wide margin. It has full-service grocery stores, multiple restaurants, a wide range of lodging from budget motels to inns, and a real downtown to spend an evening in. The 45-minute drive to the Valley is a fair trade for the amenities.

faq.q: Which Yosemite gateway towns have full grocery stores?
faq.a: Mariposa and Oakhurst have full grocery stores. Groveland has a smaller one. El Portal has a market, not a real grocery. Lee Vining has limited groceries. Stock up at the larger town before you head in.

faq.q: Do Yosemite gateway towns have reliable cell service?
faq.a: Patchy in all five gateways and essentially nonexistent inside the park past Crane Flat. Don't rely on real-time Google Maps once you're past the entrance station. Download offline maps before you leave the gateway.

title: Yosemite during smoke season: how to actually plan around it
dek: Smoke season in California now runs July through October. The question isn't whether your trip will overlap with it. It's whether you have a plan for when it does.
seoDek: Smoke season in California now runs July through October. The question isn't whether your Yosemite trip overlaps. It's whether you have a plan.
placeholder: Half Dome in profile from Washburn Point under a gray sky

faq.q: When is smoke season at Yosemite?
faq.a: Smoke season in California overlaps fire season: July through October. August and September are the most affected months on average, though clear stretches happen throughout and forecasts are unreliable beyond 48-72 hours.

faq.q: How do I check air quality before visiting Yosemite?
faq.a: AirNow.gov (EPA) is the standard source. The Yosemite NPS website posts air quality advisories. Check the AQI for the Yosemite Valley area 48-72 hours before departure and again the morning you travel, since conditions change fast during fire season.

faq.q: Should I wear a mask in Yosemite smoke?
faq.a: If AQI is above 100, an N95 or KN95 mask provides meaningful protection, especially for children, the elderly, and anyone with respiratory conditions. Surgical masks and cloth masks do not filter fine smoke particles effectively.

faq.q: What is a good alternative if Yosemite is too smoky?
faq.a: The eastern Sierra often has better air when prevailing winds carry smoke away from the park. Mono Lake, Mammoth Lakes, and the June Lake Loop are worth checking. Smoke also typically clears after cold fronts, so monitoring the forecast 48-72 hours out gives you a real window to reschedule.

faq.q: Does wildfire smoke ruin a Yosemite trip?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Haze produces warm, diffused light that photographers actively seek. On moderate-smoke days (AQI 50-100) most outdoor activities are fine for healthy adults. On heavy-smoke days (AQI 150+) strenuous outdoor exercise is not recommended, so use the time for indoor sites, driving routes, or the Ahwahnee.

title: Yosemite without reservations in 2026: a real strategy for the year the cap came off
dek: The reservation system was a throttle. With it gone in 2026, the park hasn't gotten easier. It's gotten harder. Here's the real strategy.
seoDek: The reservation cap came off for 2026. The park didn't get easier, it got harder. A real strategy for visiting Yosemite without reservations.

faq.q: Do I need a reservation to enter Yosemite in 2026?
faq.a: No. The day-use vehicle reservation system is not in effect in 2026. A standard Yosemite entrance pass is required.

faq.q: When does Yosemite Valley fill up?
faq.a: Day-use parking in the Valley typically fills by 8 a.m. on summer weekends, often closer to 7:30, and by late morning on weekdays. Tunnel View fills earlier still on peak days. Once lots are full, you're circling.

faq.q: What time should I enter Yosemite to beat the crowds?
faq.a: Be inside the park by 6:30 a.m. on any peak day. Earlier is better. The drive in is faster, the entrance stations are quiet, and you'll be parked at your first stop while everyone else is still in line.

faq.q: What changed in Yosemite in 2026?
faq.a: Two things. The day-use entrance reservation system was dropped, so the requirement that throttled vehicles during peak hours in 2024 and 2025 is gone and the park is expected to see its heaviest crowds in years. Separately, since January 1, 2026, international visitors pay a $100 surcharge per person on top of the standard entrance fee; domestic visitors are unaffected.

faq.q: How do I find parking in Yosemite Valley?
faq.a: Arrive before 8 a.m. for the central lots. Yosemite Village day-use parking is the most useful base. If those are full, the lot near Yosemite Valley Lodge usually has turnover by mid-morning. Don't waste time circling Curry Village.

faq.q: What should I figure out before planning a Yosemite trip?
faq.a: Three things, before you book anything. What season you'll visit and what's open then (Tioga Road and Glacier Point Road are closed into late spring most years). Which two of granite, waterfalls, old-growth, high country, and solitude matter most to you. And what your backup plan is when the park doesn't cooperate.

faq.q: How long should I spend in Yosemite for a first trip?
faq.a: At minimum two full days. Three to four is better, giving you time to see Yosemite Valley properly and have one full day elsewhere (Glacier Point, Mariposa Grove, or Tioga Road). A single-day trip is possible but you'll be moving constantly.

faq.q: What month is best for visiting Yosemite for the first time?
faq.a: Late May and early June for peak waterfalls; September and October for low crowds and golden light. July and August are warm and crowded. April has waterfalls and fewer people but Tioga Road and Glacier Point Road are usually still closed.

faq.q: Do I need a reservation to enter Yosemite in 2026?
faq.a: No. The day-use entrance reservation system is not in effect in 2026. A standard Yosemite entrance pass is required.

faq.q: Where should I stay on a first Yosemite trip?
faq.a: Mariposa is the most practical first-timer choice, about 45 minutes from the Valley, a real downtown, full-service. El Portal is closer (25-30 minutes) but smaller and more expensive. In-park lodging fills six to twelve months ahead.

faq.q: How much driving is involved in a Yosemite trip?
faq.a: More than most people expect. A gateway town to the Valley is 25 to 90 minutes each way. Glacier Point Road and Tioga Road each add an hour or more in-park. Pack the car like you're going to drive a lot, because you will.

faq.q: Is there an extra fee for international visitors in 2026?
faq.a: Yes. Since January 1, 2026, international visitors pay a $100 surcharge per person age 16 and older, on top of the standard entrance fee. A $250 nonresident annual pass waives it. U.S. residents are unaffected.
